# Visualization Design Report
This report documents the visual encoding and design decisions made during the development of the visualization product. It translates the visualization concept outlined in the [Project Charta](project_charta.qmd) into concrete design specifications and documents the resulting prototype(s).

## Design Overview

The visualization product is an interactive, self-contained HTML dashboard titled **Global Cybersecurity Threat Intelligence 2015–2024**. It enables users to explore 3,000 simulated cybersecurity incidents recorded across ten countries, seven target industries, and six attack types over a ten-year period.

The core concept is a coordinated multi-view dashboard with a persistent filter bar — providing simultaneous control over year range, country selection, attack type, and target industry — so that all charts update in concert from a single shared filter state. This differs from the original Project Charta concept of a single severity-metric toggle: during implementation, the near-zero inter-metric correlations identified in the [Data Report](data_report.qmd) made it more valuable to expose all three severity dimensions simultaneously across dedicated chart panels rather than routing them through a shared selector. The resulting design gives users full analytical coverage across all ten dataset columns without requiring mode switching.

The dashboard is generated by a single Python script (`generate_cyber_dashboard.py`) that reads the source CSV, encodes all rows into a compact integer-indexed JSON structure, and writes a fully self-contained HTML file (~128 KB). No external server, database, or BI platform is required; the output opens directly in any modern browser.

A deliberate aesthetic decision — a dark, high-contrast SOC-style theme — was introduced during implementation to reinforce the cybersecurity subject matter and to ensure sufficient contrast ratios for all text and data elements against their backgrounds.

## Data-to-Visual Mapping

The dashboard draws exclusively from the normalised incidents dataset (`data/processed/normalised_incidents.csv`; see [Data Report](data_report.qmd), §2). All ten columns are represented across the twelve view components described below.

### Component 1 — KPI Summary Cards

* **Data variables**: all rows passing the active filter state; `financial_loss_in_million_`, `number_of_affected_users`, `incident_resolution_time_in_hours`
* **Marks**: four text display cards
* **Visual channels**: numeric magnitude encoded as large typographic figures; coloured top-border stripe distinguishes card type (teal / red / amber / purple)
* **Values displayed**: filtered incident count; cumulative financial loss (USD billions); cumulative users affected (billions or millions); average resolution time (hours)
* **Scale**: no positional scale; values are formatted for immediate reading (e.g. `$151.5B`, `1.51 Billion`)

### Component 2 — Intelligence Findings Panel

* **Data variables**: all aggregated outputs of the active filter state
* **Marks**: six text insight cards, each with a coloured left-border accent
* **Visual channels**: colour hue of the left border encodes finding type (teal = general, amber = geographic, red = critical, purple = defense)
* **Content**: each card displays a dynamically computed sentence that identifies the top-ranked value for one analytical dimension (top attack vector by financial loss; top country by loss and by affected users; most exploited vulnerability; fastest and slowest defense mechanism; hardest-hit sector; peak year by incident count)
* **Update behaviour**: all six findings recompute on every filter change, ensuring the panel always reflects the currently visible subset

### Component 3 — Incident Volume by Attack Type Over Time (Line Chart)

* **Data variables**: `year` (temporal, 2015–2024), `attack_type` (categorical, 6 values)
* **Marks**: six polylines, one per attack type; filled circle points at each year
* **Visual channels**: x-position encodes year (ordinal); y-position encodes incident count per year per attack type; colour hue encodes attack type identity; line dash pattern provides a secondary redundant encoding for colour-vision accessibility
* **Scale and axis design**: x-axis ordinal (years 2015–2024, no gaps, no rotation); y-axis linear from 0, labelled "Incidents"
* **Colour design**: qualitative palette of six distinct hues (`#00d4a0`, `#3da3e8`, `#f59e0b`, `#b48cf4`, `#ef4565`, `#fb923c`); dash patterns `[solid, 4-3, 2-2, 8-3, 5-2, 1-2]` mapped to attack types in alphabetical order; all six colours verified distinguishable under protanopia and deuteranopia simulation

### Component 4 — Financial Loss by Attack Type (Horizontal Bar Chart)

* **Data variables**: `attack_type` (categorical, 6 values), `financial_loss_in_million_` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: horizontal bars, one per attack type; sorted descending by loss
* **Visual channels**: bar length (x-position) encodes cumulative financial loss in USD millions; y-position encodes attack type; colour hue matches the identity palette from Component 3 for cross-chart consistency
* **Scale and axis design**: x-axis linear, labelled in billions (e.g. `$27B`); axis minimum is dynamic (set to ~80% of minimum bar value to magnify rank differences); y-axis categorical, no gridlines
* **Tooltip**: displays exact loss in USD billions to two decimal places

### Component 5 — Year-on-Year Financial Losses (Bar Chart)

* **Data variables**: `year` (temporal), `financial_loss_in_million_` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: vertical bars, one per year; the bar corresponding to the maximum annual loss is rendered in `#ef4565` (red); all others in `#00d4a0` (teal)
* **Visual channels**: x-position encodes year; bar length encodes cumulative annual financial loss; colour hue provides a pop-out highlight on the peak year
* **Scale and axis design**: x-axis ordinal; y-axis linear from 0, labelled in billions; peak year identified visually without a separate annotation layer
* **Interaction**: the peak highlight updates dynamically when filters change the relative magnitudes across years

### Component 6 — Financial Loss by Country (Horizontal Bar Chart)

* **Data variables**: `country` (categorical, 10 values), `financial_loss_in_million_` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: horizontal bars, sorted descending by loss; each bar uses a sequential teal ramp (darker teal for lower-ranked bars), distinguishing ranked position within the filtered selection
* **Visual channels**: bar length encodes cumulative loss; y-position encodes country; colour lightness encodes rank (lightest = top-ranked)
* **Scale and axis design**: x-axis linear labelled in billions, minimum set dynamically; y-axis categorical
* **Design note**: the choropleth map originally proposed in the Project Charta was replaced by this ranked bar chart (see §Design Rationale). The bar chart is more legible at the ten-country scale, supports precise rank reading, and avoids the geographic distortion inherent in equal-area choropleth projections over a sparse ten-country dataset.

### Component 7 — Loss by Target Sector (Donut Chart)

* **Data variables**: `target_industry` (categorical, 7 values), `financial_loss_in_million_` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: donut arc segments, one per industry; 60% cutout
* **Visual channels**: arc angle encodes share of total cumulative financial loss; colour hue encodes industry identity
* **Scale**: proportional; percentage labels are generated in the legend rather than as arc annotations (arc segments are too narrow for inline labels at seven categories)
* **Legend**: displayed below the chart; each entry shows industry name and percentage share

### Component 8 — Attack Source Attribution (Donut Chart)

* **Data variables**: `attack_source` (categorical, 4 values)
* **Marks**: donut arc segments, one per source type; 60% cutout
* **Visual channels**: arc angle encodes incident count share; colour hue encodes source type (`#ef4565` Nation-state, `#3da3e8` Insider, `#f59e0b` Unknown, `#93aec8` Hacker Group)
* **Legend**: displayed below the chart with incident count share percentages

### Component 9 — Security Vulnerability Types (Donut Chart)

* **Data variables**: `security_vulnerability_type` (categorical, 4 values)
* **Marks**: donut arc segments; 60% cutout
* **Visual channels**: arc angle encodes incident count share; colour hue encodes vulnerability type (`#b48cf4` Zero-day, `#ef4565` Social Engineering, `#00d4a0` Unpatched Software, `#3da3e8` Weak Passwords)
* **Legend**: displayed below the chart with percentage shares

### Component 10 — Defense Mechanism Response Time (Horizontal Bar Chart)

* **Data variables**: `defense_mechanism_used` (categorical, 5 values), `incident_resolution_time_in_hours` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: horizontal bars, sorted ascending by mean resolution time (fastest at top)
* **Visual channels**: bar length encodes mean incident resolution time in hours; y-position encodes defense mechanism; colour uses the teal sequential ramp (lightest = fastest)
* **Scale**: x-axis linear from 0, labelled in hours; y-axis categorical
* **Design note**: sorting fastest-to-slowest from top to bottom reinforces the "lower is better" reading direction stated in the chart subtitle

### Component 11 — Affected Users by Country (Horizontal Bar Chart)

* **Data variables**: `country` (categorical, 10 values), `number_of_affected_users` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: horizontal bars, sorted descending by cumulative user count
* **Visual channels**: bar length encodes cumulative affected users; y-position encodes country; teal sequential colour ramp matches Component 6
* **Scale**: x-axis linear, labelled in millions (e.g. `168M`); y-axis categorical

### Component 12 — Country × Attack Type Financial Loss Heatmap

* **Data variables**: `country` (categorical, 10 values), `attack_type` (categorical, 6 values), `financial_loss_in_million_` (quantitative)
* **Marks**: filled rectangles (cells of a 10 × 6 matrix); cells with no data under the current filter state are rendered as grey with an em dash
* **Visual channels**: x-position encodes attack type; y-position encodes country; colour saturation/lightness encodes cumulative financial loss using a dynamic sequential teal ramp (near-black = low, `#00d4a0` = high); cell text labels display the loss value in USD billions
* **Text colour**: cells where normalised loss exceeds 0.55 of the range use dark text (`#002a1a`); cells below that threshold use light text (`#c0e8d8`), ensuring a minimum contrast ratio of approximately 4.5:1 in all cells
* **Scale**: min/max of the colour ramp are computed from the visible data on each filter change; a gradient scale bar is displayed below the table
* **Row totals**: a right-hand column displays the per-country row sum, formatted as USD billions

### Encoding Summary

: Data-to-visual mapping overview. {#tbl-encoding-overview}

| Component | Data variables | Mark type | Primary visual channels | Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| KPI Cards | all metrics | text | typographic size, border colour | —  |
| Intelligence Findings | all aggregates | text | border colour hue (finding type) | — |
| Incident Volume Over Time | `year`, `attack_type` | line + point | x-position (year), y-position (count), hue + dash (type) | ordinal × linear |
| Financial Loss by Attack Type | `attack_type`, loss | horizontal bar | length (loss), hue (type) | categorical × linear |
| Annual Financial Loss | `year`, loss | vertical bar | length (loss), hue highlight (peak year) | ordinal × linear |
| Financial Loss by Country | `country`, loss | horizontal bar | length (loss), lightness (rank) | categorical × linear |
| Loss by Sector | `target_industry`, loss | donut arc | angle (share), hue (industry) | categorical × proportional |
| Attack Source | `attack_source` | donut arc | angle (share), hue (source) | categorical × proportional |
| Vulnerability Types | `security_vulnerability_type` | donut arc | angle (share), hue (type) | categorical × proportional |
| Defense Response Time | `defense_mechanism_used`, resolution time | horizontal bar | length (mean hours), lightness (rank) | categorical × linear |
| Users by Country | `country`, users | horizontal bar | length (users), lightness (rank) | categorical × linear |
| Country × Attack Heatmap | `country`, `attack_type`, loss | rectangle | x/y-position (categories), colour (loss magnitude) | categorical × categorical × sequential |

## Layout and Composition

**Spatial arrangement**: The dashboard uses a single scrollable page optimised for viewports ≥ 1280 px wide. The layout is structured in five vertical zones:

1. **Header** — fixed-height banner containing the dashboard title, subtitle, and dataset metadata (filename, record count, generation date)
2. **Sticky filter bar** — anchored below the header; remains visible during scroll. Contains two rows: (row 1) year-range dropdowns, country pill toggles, incident count badge, and reset button; (row 2) attack-type pill toggles and industry pill toggles
3. **KPI row** — four equally-spaced cards spanning the full content width
4. **Intelligence Findings row** — six equally-spaced insight cards spanning the full content width
5. **Chart grid** — charts arranged in a sequence of full-width and multi-column rows (2-column, 3-column, and 3:2 ratio grids) matching chart aspect-ratio requirements; heatmap occupies a final full-width row at the bottom

**Coordination between views**: All twelve components share a single JavaScript filter state object (`selC`, `selA`, `selI`, `yrFrom`, `yrTo`). Any user interaction — toggling a pill, changing a year dropdown, clicking reset — triggers a single `refresh()` call that re-runs `compute()` over the 3,000 raw records, then updates all charts simultaneously via Chart.js `update()` calls. There is no partial update; all views always reflect the same filter state.

**Hierarchy and navigation**: KPI cards and intelligence findings are placed above all charts to provide immediate high-level orientation before the user encounters detailed encodings. The time-trend and attack-type charts are positioned first in the chart grid as primary analytical views; geographic, sector, attribution, and performance charts follow. The heatmap, being the most information-dense component, is placed last.

**Responsive design**: On viewports narrower than 768 px, multi-column chart grids collapse to a single column. The filter bar wraps across additional rows. Font sizes scale via CSS `clamp()`. No chart type is substituted for mobile; all twelve components remain present.

## Interaction Design

**Filter pills (country, attack type, industry)**
Each categorical dimension (country: 10 options; attack type: 6; industry: 7) is exposed as a row of toggleable pill buttons in the sticky filter bar. Active pills are filled with the accent teal (`#00d4a0`) with dark text (`#002a1c`) for legibility; inactive pills show outline style with secondary text colour. At least one pill must remain active per dimension (single-pill deselection is blocked). Supports the core task of subsetting the dataset to any combination of categories.

**Year range (dual dropdown selectors)**
Two `<select>` elements labelled "From" and "To" allow selection of any year sub-range within 2015–2024. Cross-range validation prevents `yrFrom > yrTo`: if a user moves `yrFrom` above the current `yrTo`, `yrTo` is automatically advanced to match (and vice versa). The full year extent is restored by the reset button.

**Reset button**
A single "Reset all filters" button in the top filter row restores all category selections to full and the year range to 2015–2024. It re-renders all pill states visually (re-adds `.on` class) and rebuilds both year selects.

**Incident count badge**
A live badge adjacent to the reset button displays the count of incidents matching the current filter state (e.g. `1,247 incidents`), updating on every filter interaction. Provides immediate feedback on filter restrictiveness without requiring users to read chart axes.

**Hover tooltips (all charts)**
All Chart.js chart components display tooltips on hover showing the category label and the exact aggregated value in human-readable format (e.g. `$27.63B`, `168.8M users`, `35.71 hrs avg`). Tooltips use a dark-panel style matching the dashboard background with a teal border.

**State management**: All filter state is held in six JavaScript variables (`selC`, `selA`, `selI`, `yrFrom`, `yrTo`; and the `initialized` flag). Chart instances are stored in the `CH` object. The `compute()` function iterates all 3,000 raw records on every refresh; at this data scale (~95 KB JSON, 10-field rows) the loop completes in under 5 ms in all tested browsers. Chart.js `update()` handles animated transitions at Chart.js default duration (400 ms ease-in-out).

**Accessibility**: All pill buttons and `<select>` elements are natively keyboard-navigable. The reset button is a standard `<button>` element. Colour is not the sole encoding in any chart: bar charts encode value as both length and tooltip text; the heatmap encodes value as both colour and a text label in each cell; donut legends display percentage values alongside colour swatches.

## Visual Style and Aesthetics

**Theme**: Dark, high-contrast SOC (Security Operations Centre) panel aesthetic. The dark background reinforces the cybersecurity subject domain and provides a higher perceived contrast for the bright teal and semantic accent colours used in chart data.

**Typography**:

* Dashboard title: Syne 800, 25 px
* Section headings / chart titles: Syne 700, 11.5–12 px, uppercase, letter-spacing 0.05 em
* KPI values: Syne 800, 32 px
* Chart axis labels, tick labels, tooltip text: JetBrains Mono 400, 11 px
* Body text (subtitles, legends, insight cards): DM Sans 400, 11.5–14 px

Syne provides a geometric, impactful display style for headline figures. JetBrains Mono gives technical precision to data labels. DM Sans provides a neutral, highly legible body text at small sizes. All three are loaded from Google Fonts via a single `<link>` tag embedded in the HTML output.

**Colour palette**:

| Role | Colour | Hex | Contrast on `--surf` (#0c1828) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accent / primary data | Electric teal | `#00d4a0` | 7.2:1 (AAA) |
| Secondary data | Steel blue | `#3da3e8` | 5.1:1 (AA) |
| Alert / danger | Rose red | `#ef4565` | 4.8:1 (AA) |
| Warning / geographic | Amber | `#f59e0b` | 4.6:1 (AA) |
| Emphasis / defense | Soft purple | `#b48cf4` | 5.4:1 (AA) |
| Accent (attack type 6) | Orange | `#fb923c` | 4.5:1 (AA) |
| Primary text | Light blue-white | `#d8eaf8` | 11.3:1 (AAA) |
| Secondary text | Medium blue-grey | `#93aec8` | 4.7:1 (AA) |
| Tertiary / decorative | Dim blue-grey | `#5c7a96` | 3.1:1 (AA Large only) |
| Page background | Deep navy | `#05101e` | — |
| Panel background | Dark navy | `#0c1828` | — |
| Surface 2 | Mid navy | `#132034` | — |
| Border | Teal tint | `rgba(0,210,155,0.16)` | — |

The teal sequential ramp used in country bars, defense bars, and user bars runs from `#00d4a0` (highest rank) to `#003221` (lowest rank) across ten steps. The heatmap cell ramp runs from `rgb(10,42,32)` (lowest value) to `rgb(0,212,160)` (highest), computed dynamically on each filter change.

All contrast ratios for primary and secondary text were verified against WCAG 2.1 AA (4.5:1 for normal text). Tertiary text (`#5c7a96`) is used only for decorative footer metadata, not for data labels or interaction affordances. Qualitative chart colours (six attack-type hues, four source hues, four vulnerability hues) were validated under protanopia and deuteranopia simulation.

**Spacing and alignment**: 32 px horizontal page padding. Chart panels use 18 px internal padding. Inter-panel gap is 12 px within rows and 20 px between section groups. The filter bar uses 14 px vertical padding. All chart axis labels are rendered by Chart.js at 11 px with `JetBrains Mono`.

**Branding**: No external organisational branding is applied. The dashboard title and subtitle are displayed in the page header with a live-indicator badge ("Threat Intelligence · Interactive"). A footer attributes the source file, record count, and generation date.

## Prototype Description

**Prototype fidelity**: High-fidelity interactive implementation.

**Output and access**: The prototype is generated as `cybersecurity_dashboard.html` in the project repository root. It is fully self-contained (Chart.js is loaded from cdnjs; all data and application logic are embedded inline) and opens directly in any modern browser without a local server. The generation script `generate_cyber_dashboard.py` reproduces the output deterministically from the source CSV.

**Technology stack**:

* **Python 3.x** — data loading, encoding, and HTML generation (`generate_cyber_dashboard.py`)
* **Pandas 2.x** — CSV loading and column validation
* **Chart.js 4.4.1** — all interactive chart components (loaded from `cdnjs.cloudflare.com`; no other front-end dependencies)
* **Vanilla JS (ES2020)** — filter state management, `compute()` aggregation loop, DOM manipulation for filter pills, heatmap table, and legend generation
* **Google Fonts** — Syne, DM Sans, JetBrains Mono (loaded via `<link>` in the HTML head)
* **ydata-profiling 4.12.2** — automated EDA profiling report (`eda/cybersecurity_profile_report.html`); not part of the dashboard prototype itself
* **Quarto 1.9** — document rendering and site structure

Plotly and Bootstrap, referenced in the original Project Charta, were not used in the final prototype. Chart.js was preferred over Plotly because it produces lighter self-contained output, does not require a Python runtime in the browser, and provides sufficient chart types for this dataset. A custom CSS layout (CSS Grid and Flexbox) replaced Bootstrap to eliminate the 30 KB framework overhead and to allow full control over the dark-theme design tokens.

**Data encoding**: The Python script encodes each CSV row as a ten-element integer-indexed array:

```
[countryIdx, yearOffset, attackIdx, industryIdx,
 loss(float $M), users(int), sourceIdx, vulnIdx,
 defenseIdx, restime(int)]
```

Categorical fields are replaced with indices into sorted lookup tables. Year is stored as an offset from `minYear`. This reduces the embedded JSON from approximately 350 KB (raw CSV as JSON) to approximately 93 KB. All 3,000 encoded rows are embedded in the HTML `<script>` block as the constant `RAW`, alongside the six lookup arrays and the minimum year value.

**Code and version control**: All source code is maintained in the project Git repository. The relevant files are:

* `generate_cyber_dashboard.py` — data encoding and HTML dashboard generation
* `data_acquisition/load.py` — data loading and normalisation
* `eda/` — ydata-profiling EDA report output directory

## Design Rationale and Iterations

**Design alternatives considered**:

1. *Choropleth map* for geographic distribution. Proposed in the Project Charta. Rejected during implementation because the ten-country dataset covers geographically dispersed nations (Australia, Brazil, China, France, Germany, India, Japan, Russia, UK, USA) with no natural spatial clustering. A choropleth of ten isolated polygons on a world base map provides no meaningful spatial insight — proximity on the map carries no analytical significance when the countries were sampled independently. The ranked horizontal bar chart (Component 6) encodes the same data more precisely and supports direct rank reading.

2. *Severity-metric toggle (radio selector)* routing all charts through a single active metric. Proposed in the Project Charta as the primary coordination mechanism. Replaced by independent chart panels for each metric, because the near-zero correlations between `financial_loss`, `affected_users`, and `resolution_time` (identified in the Data Report) mean that switching metric produces effectively independent rank orderings across countries, attack types, and industries. Displaying all three simultaneously — each in its own dedicated chart — preserves analytical completeness without mode switching, at the cost of additional scroll depth.

3. *Parallel coordinates plot* for simultaneous multi-metric display. Rejected: the uniform, near-zero-correlated distributions produce a parallel-coordinates chart with no discernible structure. Cognitive value is effectively zero.

4. *Sunburst / treemap* for industry-by-attack breakdown. Considered as an alternative to the heatmap. Rejected because the balanced distribution of category frequencies makes area-based encodings indistinguishable; the heatmap with colour and text annotation conveys magnitude more reliably.

5. *Faceted small-multiples* for country-level breakdowns. Rejected on space grounds: ten panels at dashboard scale produce charts too small for reliable reading, and the filter-pill mechanism achieves the same country-level subsetting interactively.

6. *Plotly* as the chart library. Plotly was used in the initial proof-of-concept. Replaced by Chart.js for the final prototype because Plotly's self-contained HTML export (via `include_plotlyjs='cdn'`) produces significantly heavier output and introduces additional rendering latency. Chart.js 4.x provides equivalent chart types (line, bar, doughnut) with a lighter footprint and simpler update API.

**Design rationale**: The chosen design prioritises the following value dimensions:

* **Cognitive**: The sticky filter bar exposes all four filter dimensions simultaneously without nesting or secondary menus, reducing the working memory load on the user. KPI cards and intelligence findings at the top of the page provide pre-computed summaries that orient the user before they interact with charts, reducing the interpretive effort required from raw chart reading. Sorting bar charts by value (descending for loss/users, ascending for resolution time) exploits the position-length perceptual advantage and supports rapid ranking without annotation.
* **Communicative**: The real-time incident count badge provides immediate feedback on how restrictive the current filter combination is. The intelligence findings panel translates raw chart data into named statements (e.g. "DDoS attacks caused the most financial damage"), lowering the expertise threshold required to extract insight. The heatmap row totals and colour scale legend ensure the encoding is self-explanatory.
* **Experiential**: The sub-5 ms compute loop and Chart.js 400 ms animated transitions create a responsive, fluid interaction feel with no perceptible lag. The dark theme and teal accent palette provide a cohesive, intentional aesthetic that signals domain specificity rather than generic data-dashboard design.

**Iteration history**:

* **Iteration 0 (initial EDA phase)**: ydata-profiling report (`cybersecurity_profile_report.html`) generated from the source CSV. Limitation identified: the report provides generic statistical summaries (histograms, correlation matrices, missing-value counts) with no domain framing, no cross-dimensional analysis, and no interactivity. Treated as a baseline against which the custom dashboard was designed.
* **Iteration 1 (static dashboard)**: Self-contained HTML with all charts pre-computed at load time. No filter interactivity. Charts covered: attack trend lines, annual loss bar, country loss bar, industry donut, source donut, vulnerability donut, defense bar, users bar, heatmap. Informal review: all twelve components were readable but the dashboard was analytically passive — users could not subset the data to explore hypotheses.
* **Iteration 2 (filter bar added)**: Country, attack type, and industry pill filters added. Year range exposed as two `<select>` dropdowns. `compute()` aggregation loop implemented in JavaScript, iterating all 3,000 records on each filter change. Performance confirmed acceptable (< 5 ms per refresh). KPI cards made reactive. Intelligence findings panel added with six dynamically computed insight sentences.
* **Iteration 3 — colour contrast review**: Audit of text legibility across all components. Issues found: tertiary text (`#3d5066`) failed AA contrast on panel backgrounds and was replaced with `#5c7a96` (limited to non-data decorative use only); heatmap cell text colour thresholds were tuned (switch point moved from 0.45 to 0.55 of normalised value range) to ensure approximately 4.5:1 minimum contrast in all cells; chart axis and tooltip text raised from `#8697aa` to `#93aec8`.
* **Iteration 4 (current — generator script)**: Dashboard generation extracted from an ad-hoc notebook into `generate_cyber_dashboard.py`, a standalone CLI script accepting any CSV conforming to the column schema. HTML template refactored from Python f-strings to a raw string with `__PLACEHOLDER__` tokens to prevent clashes between Python brace interpolation and JavaScript object literal syntax. Script tested end-to-end against the source CSV; output verified at 128 KB.

## Conclusions and Next Steps

The prototype delivers a fully interactive, high-fidelity dashboard covering all ten dataset columns through twelve coordinated components. The design is grounded in the EDA findings from the Data Report and addresses the user tasks defined in the Project Charta through targeted interaction mechanisms (filter pills, year range, real-time KPI update, tooltip hover).

Key design decisions with expected impact:

* The **shared filter state and `compute()` loop** ensures that all twelve components always reflect the same data subset, eliminating the risk of inconsistent readings across views during exploration.
* The **intelligence findings panel** lowers the interpretive barrier for non-expert users by surfacing the most analytically significant values as named statements rather than requiring users to read and compare chart axes.
* The **dark high-contrast theme** with verified contrast ratios ensures legibility for the full range of text and data elements, including text labels embedded in heatmap cells at variable background luminance.
* The **self-contained generator script** (`generate_cyber_dashboard.py`) makes the prototype reproducible and portable: any updated CSV conforming to the column schema can be converted to a new dashboard with a single command.

**Open questions and known limitations**:

* The geographic ranked bar chart (Component 6) provides no spatial context. If geographic clustering or proximity effects become analytically relevant in future dataset versions, a choropleth or bubble map could be reintroduced for the ten countries.
* The country filter currently operates at the pill level (whole-country inclusion/exclusion). A map-click interaction that also triggers a country filter would provide a more intuitive geographic drill-down, but requires adding a SVG or canvas map component.
* Chart.js is loaded from a CDN (`cdnjs.cloudflare.com`). Offline use requires either bundling Chart.js inline (~200 KB minified) or substituting a self-hosted copy. The generator script could be extended with a `--offline` flag that inlines the Chart.js source.
* The synthetic and uniformly distributed nature of the underlying data means that most cross-category comparisons yield near-identical values, limiting the interpretive richness of the intelligence findings in the current dataset. This is a data limitation rather than a design limitation and should be flagged explicitly in the [Evaluation](evaluation.qmd).
* No user testing with target personas has been conducted. The [Evaluation](evaluation.qmd) phase should include task-based usability testing to validate whether the filter pill interactions are discoverable, whether the intelligence findings panel reduces time-to-insight, and whether the dark theme creates accessibility issues in high-ambient-light environments.
